title: The Book of the Grotesque & Hands
paragraphs: THE BOOK OF THE GROTESQUE

The writer, an old man with a white mustache, had some difficulty in getting into bed. The windows of the house in which he lived were high and he wanted to look at the trees when he awoke in the morning. A carpenter came to fix the bed so that it would be on a level with the window.

Quite a fuss was made about the matter. The carpenter, who had been a soldier in the Civil War, came into the writer's room and sat down to talk of building a platform for the purpose of raising the bed. The writer had cigars lying about and the carpenter smoked.

For a long time the two men talked of the raising of the bed and then they talked of other things. The soldier got on the subject of the war. He had once been on a long march and had got lost in a forest. He described the feeling he had as he wandered. The writer, listening, became absorbed.

After the soldier went away, the writer lay in his bed and thought of the soldiers and the long march. It was not a comfortable thought. The writer had been made somewhat ill by the words of the soldier.

He had a theory about the grotesque. It was like this: All through the world there had been many thoughts and the thoughts were all beautiful. The thoughts came to life—became truths. Then a terrible thing happened. The moment one of the people took one of the truths to himself, called it his truth, and tried to live his life by it, he became a grotesque and the truth he embraced became a falsehood.
HANDS

Upon the half decayed veranda of a small frame house that stood near the edge of a ravine near the town of Winesburg, Ohio, a fat little old man walked nervously up and down. His name was Wing Biddlebaum. He was afraid of the hands.

The hands alarmed their owner. He wanted to keep them hidden away and looked with amazement at the quiet inexpressive hands of other men who worked beside him in the fields, or passed, driving sleepy teams on country roads.

Wing Biddlebaum's hands were forever active, forever striving to conceal themselves in his pockets or behind his back. As he walked in the evening on the crumbling veranda, they were continually flitting up and down, scratching at the hair of his head or rubbing his face.

When he talked to George Willard, the reporter for the Winesburg Eagle, the hands were quiet. Seeing the boy the old man seemed to feel something he had lost. He would talk and forget his hands and then George Willard would go away and the hands would begin their restless movement.

The story of Wing Biddlebaum is a story of hands. Their restless activity, like unto the beating of the wings of an imprisoned bird, had given him his name. Some obscure poet of the town had thought of it.

In his youth Wing Biddlebaum had been a schoolteacher in a town in Pennsylvania. He had loved his boys and touched them with his hands. And then a half-witted boy in the town had whispered things he had dreamed, and a nightmare had come upon Wing Biddlebaum. With lanterns in their hands a crowd of men had come to the door of his house and told him to go.

He had come to Winesburg. Twenty years he had lived here. And in all that time no one had come close enough to him to see that when he talked his hands fluttered. In the presence of George Willard, Wing Biddlebaum, who for twenty years had been the town mystery, lost something of his timidity, and his shadowy personality, submerged in a sea of doubts, came forth to look at the world.
"You must begin to dream," he said to the boy. "You must try to forget all you have learned."

Then his hands came forward and touched the shoulders of George Willard, and he was terrified. With a convulsive movement of his body, Wing Biddlebaum sprang to his feet and thrust his hands deep into his trouser pockets. Tears came to his eyes.
"I must be getting along home. I can talk no more with you," he said nervously.

title: Mother & The Philosopher
paragraphs: MOTHER

Elizabeth Willard, the mother of George Willard, was a tall woman of twenty-two when her son was born. She had been a girl of eighteen when her father arranged for her marriage to Tom Willard. She remembered little of her youth. In the years following her marriage and before the time of her son's birth she had tried hard to make something of her life.

Elizabeth Willard had a secret. She kept it from her son and from her husband. She kept it even from herself, most of the time, but sometimes in the evenings when she lay alone in her room in the New Willard House, her husband asleep and the boy George asleep and the town quiet, she would take the secret out and look at it.

The secret was this: she had wanted to be an actress. When she was a girl she had dreamed of it—of going away, of becoming someone else, of putting on other voices and other faces and other lives. But her father had arranged the marriage, and she had married Tom Willard, and the hotel had become her life.

She watched her son George. He was restless too. She could see it in the way he walked and the way he looked at things. She was afraid for him—afraid that the town would swallow him as it had swallowed her—and she was also proud of his restlessness, because it was hers.

One evening, sitting in her room above the hotel, she decided to give him the one thing she had to give: twenty-two dollars she had saved, coin by coin, for years. It was everything. She would give it to him and tell him: go. Get out of Winesburg. Live the life I couldn't.
She rehearsed what she would say. But when the moment came to speak, she found she could say nothing. She handed him the money without explanation. And he took it without understanding what it meant.
THE PHILOSOPHER

Doctor Parcival was a man with a large head and a great shock of grey hair. He was known in Winesburg as the man who seldom worked and who talked when any listener appeared. George Willard was his most frequent listener.
"I have a philosophy," said Doctor Parcival to George Willard one day. "I want to explain it to you, and then I want you to go away and think about it."
George sat in a chair by the doctor's office window and watched the people in the street.

"My philosophy is this," said Doctor Parcival. "Everyone in the world is Christ, and they are all crucified. That's my philosophy. I thought it out one night, walking in the rain, and I haven't been able to shake it since."
George did not know what to say. He looked at the doctor's large, troubled face.

"The moment people realize this," continued the doctor, "everything will be different. Or maybe not. Maybe they already know it and it makes no difference. I don't know. I sit here all day and I think about it, and I don't know."
He was quiet for a while. Then: "Write about me when I'm dead. Will you?"
"Why would I write about you?" asked George.
"Because I've lived in this town my whole life and no one has ever understood me," said the doctor. "That makes me worth writing about."

title: Loneliness & Departure
paragraphs: LONELINESS

Enoch Robinson was a small man with greying hair and a foolish look about his face. He was in his mid-fifties, but he seemed both younger and older at the same time—younger in the way he moved, older in his eyes.

In his twenties he had gone to New York to study art. He had lived in a room in New York and had painted and thought. But he was lonely. He made friends but he could not talk to his friends—could not make them understand what he was trying to say. So he stopped trying.

He invented companions. He filled his room with imaginary people—men and women and children who understood him completely, who listened to him and never misunderstood and never went away. He lived with them for years.

Then a real woman came into his life. She was a model. She was kind and interested. She asked him questions about his paintings and he began to answer—began to talk to her as he had never talked to anyone real.

One day he saw something terrible in her eyes. She was understanding him. She was taking hold of his things, making them hers. And suddenly he was afraid—afraid of being understood, of being known, of having his private world entered.
He told her to go. She left, and with her she took, somehow, all his imaginary people. His room was empty after that—really empty.
He came back to Winesburg. He sat in his room in the Willard house and looked out the window at the street and said nothing to anyone.
DEPARTURE
Young George Willard was about to leave Winesburg for the city. He was going to a city newspaper. He had twenty-two dollars—his mother's money—and a bag.

In the morning before he left he walked through the town. He walked past the houses of people he knew—Doctor Reefy and Wing Biddlebaum and all the rest. He wanted to say something to them, but he did not know what, and they were not expecting him, and so he just walked past.
At the station he waited for the train. Helen White, the banker's daughter, whom he had loved in his careless, seventeen-year-old way, passed on the road. She did not stop. He did not call to her.
The train came. He got on. The town began to move past the windows.
"I must think about it," he said to himself. "I will think about it carefully."
He was thinking about the people he had known, the grotesques—the people with their one truth that had become a falsehood. He was going away from them. He was going toward something.
The farm fields rolled past outside. The town was gone.
George Willard had become a man. He was not sure what that meant, but he felt it—felt something close, something ending, and something else beginning, not yet clear.
He was nineteen years old and on his way to the city. The town of Winesburg had done what towns do: it had held him and marked him and let him go.
